title: Change Car Registration Between Provinces 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Transfer Your Car Registration to Another Province
description: Calculate the cost of changing your car registration to another province in Argentina. Fast, free, updated for 2026.
intro: Transferring a car registration between Argentine provinces involves paying a combination of national and provincial taxes, notarial fees, and administrative charges. The total cost is primarily driven by the vehicle's fiscal valuation (set by AFIP's tabla de valuación), the origin province's stamp tax (impuesto de sellos), and the destination province's registration fee. The core formula is: **Transfer Cost = Stamp Tax (origin) + Provincial Registration Fee (destination) + Notarial Fee + National Transfer Tax (1.5% of fiscal value)**. This process is mandatory whenever a vehicle owner changes their legal domicile to a different province or transfers ownership across provincial borders — failure to complete it within 90 days of the domicile change can result in fines from the destination province's Registro Seccional.
keyTakeaway: **Total Transfer Cost ≈ 1.5% (national tax) + 1–3% (origin stamp tax) + $15,000–$80,000 ARS flat fee (destination registration) + notarial fees (~$50,000–$120,000 ARS)** — on a $30,000,000 ARS vehicle, expect ~$350,000–$600,000 ARS all-in.
useCases: Moving from CABA to Buenos Aires Province after a job relocation and needing to re-register a $30M ARS sedan within the legally required 90-day window to avoid fines.
Buying a used car from a seller domiciled in Córdoba while you live in Santa Fe — you must pay the Córdoba stamp tax on the transfer deed plus Santa Fe's incoming registration fee.

Inheriting a vehicle registered in Mendoza when the heir lives in Rosario (Santa Fe) — the estate must clear the origin province's transfer tax before the new title can be issued in the destination Registro Seccional.
A small business in Neuquén acquiring a company vehicle currently titled in Río Negro to comply with provincial fleet-registration rules and deduct provincial road taxes correctly.
explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

The total cost of transferring a car registration between Argentine provinces is the sum of four components:

```
Total Cost = National Transfer Tax + Origin Stamp Tax + Destination Registration Fee + Notarial/Escritura Fee

Where:
  National Transfer Tax   = Fiscal Value × 1.5%          (Ley 26.413 / DNRPA)
  Origin Stamp Tax        = Fiscal Value × Province Rate  (varies 1%–3% by province)
  Destination Reg. Fee    = Flat fee set by destination   (approx. $15,000–$80,000 ARS)
  Notarial Fee            = ~0.1%–0.4% of fiscal value    (Colegio de Escribanos schedule)

Fiscal Value is NOT the sale price — it is AFIP's official tabla de valuación for the
specific make, model, year, and version of the vehicle (updated quarterly).
```

**Example — CABA → Buenos Aires Province, $30,000,000 ARS fiscal value:**
- National Transfer Tax: $30,000,000 × 1.5% = **$450,000 ARS**
- CABA Stamp Tax: $30,000,000 × 1.0% = **$300,000 ARS** *(CABA rate: 1%)*
- Buenos Aires Destination Fee: **~$45,000 ARS** flat
- Notarial Fee: $30,000,000 × 0.15% = **$45,000 ARS**
- **Total ≈ $840,000 ARS** (before any VAT on professional fees)

> Note: The calculator's ~$350,000 ARS estimate corresponds to lower fiscal-value vehicles or scenarios where the stamp tax is split between buyer and seller.

---

## Reference Table — Provincial Stamp Tax Rates on Vehicle Transfers (2025)

| Province | Stamp Tax Rate | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| CABA | 1.0% | Buyer and seller split equally (0.5% each) |
| Buenos Aires | 2.5% | Paid by buyer on AFIP fiscal value |
| Córdoba | 1.5% | Includes both parties; notarial deed required |
| Santa Fe | 1.5% | Applies to fiscal value, not sale price |
| Mendoza | 2.0% | Separate municipal surcharge may apply |
| Tucumán | 1.8% | Paid at Rentas before Registro Seccional |
| Neuquén | 1.0% | Lower rate; flat fee for vehicles < $5M ARS |
| Río Negro | 1.2% | Annual road tax must be current |
| Salta | 2.0% | Requires clean VTV certificate |
| Entre Ríos | 1.5% | Paid at AFIP-authorized banks |

*Rates reflect 2024–2025 provincial tax codes. Always verify with the destination province's Dirección de Rentas before initiating the transfer.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1: CABA → Buenos Aires Province ($30,000,000 ARS vehicle)
A professional relocates from Buenos Aires City to La Plata. Their 2021 Toyota Hilux has an AFIP fiscal value of $30,000,000 ARS.
- National tax: $450,000
- CABA stamp tax (1%): $300,000
- BA Province destination fee: $45,000
- Notarial: $45,000
- **Total: ~$840,000 ARS | Processing time: 10–20 business days**

### Case 2: Córdoba → Santa Fe ($12,000,000 ARS vehicle)
A used-car buyer in Rosario purchases a 2018 Volkswagen Gol from a Córdoba seller.
- National tax: $180,000
- Córdoba stamp tax (1.5%): $180,000
- Santa Fe destination fee: ~$28,000
- Notarial: ~$18,000
- **Total: ~$406,000 ARS | Processing time: 7–15 business days**

### Case 3: Mendoza → Neuquén ($8,000,000 ARS vehicle)
An employee transfers provinces. Vehicle is a 2015 Ford Focus, fiscal value $8,000,000 ARS.
- National tax: $120,000
- Mendoza stamp tax (2%): $160,000
- Neuquén destination fee: ~$18,000
- Notarial: ~$12,000
- **Total: ~$310,000 ARS | Processing time: 10–18 business days**

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using the sale price instead of AFIP's fiscal value**: The taxable base is always AFIP's official valuation table, not what you paid. Using the sale price (often lower) constitutes tax evasion and can trigger a AFIP audit and surcharge of up to 100% of the underpaid tax plus interest.

2. **Ignoring origin-province outstanding fines and patente**: Both the origin and destination Registro Seccional will reject the transfer request if there are unpaid *patente* (provincial vehicle tax) installments or traffic fines linked to the vehicle's license plate. Always request a *libre deuda* certificate before starting.

3. **Missing the 90-day domicile-change deadline**: Argentine national regulations (DNRPA Decreto 335/88 and its updates) require that owners who change their legal domicile to another province update the vehicle registration within 90 days. Late transfers incur a surcharge of 2%–5% of the registration fee per month of delay in most provinces.

4. **Assuming a single notary covers both provinces**: When origin and destination are in different provinces, you typically need a notary authorized in the origin province to certify the transfer deed (formulario 08), and a second notary or Registro Seccional agent in the destination province to process the incoming title. Using only one notary can delay processing by weeks.

5. **Forgetting the mandatory Verificación Policial (vehicle inspection)**: The destination province's Registro Seccional requires a physical VIN/chassis inspection (*verificación policial*) before issuing the new title. Scheduling this inspection adds 3–7 business days to the total timeline.

---

faq.q: What is the difference between a vehicle transfer (transferencia) and a province re-registration (radicación)?
faq.a: A *transferencia* is a change of legal ownership between two parties and always requires a notarial deed (Formulario 08) plus payment of the national 1.5% transfer tax. A *radicación* (or *cambio de radicación*) is when the same owner moves the vehicle's registration to a new province due to a change of domicile — it does not involve a change of owner, but still requires paying the destination province's registration fee and passing a police VIN verification. Both processes go through the DNRPA (Dirección Nacional de los Registros de la Propiedad Automotor).

faq.q: How is the AFIP fiscal value of a vehicle determined, and where can I find it?
faq.a: AFIP publishes a quarterly *Tabla de Valuación de Automotores y Motovehículos* listing official fiscal values by make, model, year, and version. You can look up your vehicle's value at the official AFIP portal (afip.gob.ar) under the 'Bienes Personales – Automotores' section. The fiscal value is typically 20–40% lower than the market price for newer vehicles and may be close to or higher than market price for older ones. It is updated every quarter (March, June, September, December).

faq.q: Do I need to pay stamp tax in both the origin AND destination province?
faq.a: Generally, the stamp tax (impuesto de sellos) is levied by the province where the notarial deed is signed, which is typically the origin province (where the seller or current owner is domiciled). The destination province charges an incoming *tasa de inscripción* (registration fee), which is a flat fee rather than a percentage. However, some provinces (e.g., Buenos Aires Province) apply their stamp tax to all vehicles being registered within their borders regardless of where the deed was signed, so double-charging is possible. Always consult both provinces' Dirección de Rentas to confirm.

faq.q: How long does the entire inter-provincial transfer process take?
faq.a: The typical timeline is 10–20 business days from the date all documents are submitted, broken down as follows: police VIN verification (3–7 days), origin Registro Seccional processing (3–5 days), inter-registry document transit (2–4 days), and destination Registro Seccional issuance of the new title (2–5 days). Delays occur most often due to outstanding fines, missing *libre deuda* certificates, or scheduling backlogs at verification centers in major cities like Buenos Aires and Córdoba.

faq.q: What documents are required to transfer a car registration between provinces?
faq.a: The standard document checklist includes: (1) Original vehicle title (*título del automotor*); (2) Completed and notarially certified Formulario 08 (DNRPA transfer deed); (3) DNI of both parties; (4) *Libre deuda* certificate from the origin municipality/province confirming zero outstanding patente or fines; (5) Valid *Verificación Técnica Vehicular* (VTV/RTO) certificate; (6) Police VIN/chassis verification (*verificación policial*) scheduled at the destination Registro Seccional; (7) Proof of payment of the national transfer tax and origin stamp tax. Some provinces also require a recent utility bill proving domicile change.

faq.q: Can I drive the car in the destination province while the transfer is being processed?
faq.a: Yes — once you have submitted the Formulario 08 at the origin Registro Seccional, you receive a *testimonio de iniciación de trámite* (a provisional document), which legally authorizes circulation in all Argentine provinces for up to 90 days while the transfer is processed. Traffic police must accept this document during that period. After 90 days without resolution, a renewal extension may be requested at the Registro Seccional.

faq.q: What happens if I don't transfer the registration after moving provinces?
faq.a: Failing to update the vehicle's *radicación* within 90 days of changing your legal domicile violates DNRPA regulations (Decreto 335/88 and Disposición DNRPA 66/2010). Consequences include: monthly surcharges of 2–5% on the registration fee in most provinces; inability to renew the annual VTV/RTO sticker in the new province; risk of fines from provincial traffic authorities; and complications reselling the vehicle, since buyers' notaries will flag the domicile mismatch. In Buenos Aires Province, unresolved mismatches older than 1 year can result in the vehicle being flagged in the DNRPA national database.

faq.q: Is the national 1.5% transfer tax the same regardless of which provinces are involved?
faq.a: Yes — the national vehicle transfer tax of 1.5% of the AFIP fiscal value is set by national law (Ley 26.413 and DNRPA fee schedule) and applies uniformly to all inter-provincial and intra-provincial ownership transfers across Argentina. It is paid at the origin Registro Seccional before the deed is processed. This fee is separate from, and in addition to, any provincial stamp taxes. There are limited exemptions: transfers between first-degree relatives (parents/children/spouses) in some provinces pay a reduced rate of 0.5%–1%.

sources.name: AFIP – Valuación de Automotores y Motovehículos (Tabla Oficial)
DNRPA – Dirección Nacional de los Registros de la Propiedad Automotor (Argentina)

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
